ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a broccoli food product entry; the stir-fried preparation with garlic is not individually coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 codes broccoli as a raw vegetable (A00LC). The stir-fried preparation with garlic and soy sauce is not separately coded; closest ingredient-level anchor used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for stir-fried broccoli with garlic as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard side dish portion of broccoli with garlic is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a small bowl or side plate of stir-fried broccoli florets as served in a Chinese restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Broccoli, stir-fried / cooked vegetables with garlic
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for cooked broccoli and stir-fried vegetable dishes. The input record values (energy 75 kcal, protein 2.5 g, fat 5.0 g, carbohydrate 5.0 g, sodium 225 mg per 100 g) are consistent with HPB-range values for stir-fried broccoli with garlic and soy sauce. Values retained and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for broccoli cooked (ID 11091) with adjustment for added oil and soy sauce. Sodium elevated above plain cooked broccoli due to soy sauce addition.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for stir-fried broccoli with garlic. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for broccoli (GI ~15, low-GI non-starchy vegetable). Stir-frying with garlic and small amounts of soy sauce does not materially alter the GI of a predominantly non-starchy vegetable. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: (15 × 5.0 g available carbohydrate per 100 g × 2) / 100 = 1.5 g GL, confirming very low glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 225 mg per 100 g from soy sauce and oyster sauce additions
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving contributes approximately 450 mg sodium, which is 20–23% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; request low-sodium soy sauce and minimal oyster sauce to reduce sodium load, and note that broccoli's potassium content (~290 mg/100 g) provides a partial DASH-diet offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.0 g/100 g), low GI (~15), and 2.0 g/100 g dietary fibre moderating glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Broccoli with garlic is a low-GI, high-fibre vegetable dish with only 10 g total carbohydrate per 200 g serving and a glycemic load of approximately 1.5, making it an excellent choice for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes; oyster sauce should be requested in minimal quantities to limit added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.75 g/100 g), zero trans fat, high vitamin C (50 mg/100 g), and sulforaphane from broccoli with anti-atherogenic properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat and trans fat, and broccoli provides sulforaphane and vitamin C with established cardioprotective antioxidant activity; sodium from soy sauce should be moderated to support blood pressure targets in cardiovascular disease management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sulforaphane and allicin with anti-inflammatory properties; vitamin C at 50 mg/100 g as antioxidant support
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Broccoli's sulforaphane and garlic's allicin provide anti-inflammatory benefits relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; use low-sodium soy sauce to avoid sodium-driven tissue swelling and specify a non-palm vegetable oil.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Broccoli with Garlic (蒜蓉西兰花, Suàn Róng Xī Lán Huā) is a classic Chinese stir-fried vegetable side dish widely served in Singapore's Chinese restaurants, zi char stalls, and hawker centres. The dish consists of fresh broccoli florets blanched briefly and then stir-fried over high heat with minced or sliced garlic in vegetable oil, typically seasoned with light soy sauce and sometimes a small amount of oyster sauce, producing a savoury, lightly garlicky flavour with a tender-crisp texture. The high-heat wok technique preserves the bright green colour and retains much of the broccoli's vitamin C content. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 75 kcal, 2.5 g protein, 5.0 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), 5.0 g carbohydrate, 2.0 g dietary fibre, and 225 mg sodium. It is a low-GI food (GI ~15) with a glycemic load of approximately 1.5 per 200 g serving, making it highly suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is rich in vitamin C (~50 mg/100 g) and contains sulforaphane, a glucosinolate-derived compound with anti-inflammatory and potential anti-atherogenic properties. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis; sodium should be moderated for hypertensive individuals by requesting low-sodium soy sauce. It is vegetarian and dairy-free, though not strictly vegan if oyster sauce is used.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo ranges for stir-fried broccoli and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked broccoli with adjustment for added oil and soy sauce. No exact HPB entry for this specific dish was identified; values are adapted from the closest approved-source equivalents. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for broccoli. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for stir-fried broccoli with garlic is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this preparation as of 2024. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific stir-fried preparation is not reported in the approved sources consulted. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this specific stir-fried preparation in the approved sources consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible — no hydrogenated fats used in standard preparation; consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for vegetable oil stir-fry. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — dish contains no animal-derived fat; consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for plant-based stir-fry. potassium_mg value of 290 mg/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked broccoli (ID 11091, ~293 mg/100 g) and is consistent with the input record's clinical note. Dietary tags: marked vegetarian=true; vegan=false because oyster sauce (animal-derived) is a common ingredient; halal=true as standard preparation uses vegetable oil and plant ingredients. Allergen: soy listed due to soy sauce in standard preparation.
